← All posts March 19, 2026

The nested radical that equals exactly 2

  • math
  • analysis
  • sequences

A deceptively innocent-looking problem from the worksheet:

Find the value of 2+2+2+2+⋯\sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \cdots}}}}2+2+2+2+⋯​​​​

The tempting shortcut is to call the whole thing LLL, write L=2+LL = \sqrt{2 + L}L=2+L​, solve L2−L−2=0L^2 - L - 2 = 0L2−L−2=0, and announce L=2L = 2L=2. But that argument silently assumes the limit exists — and nested-radical expressions are exactly the kind of object where that assumption can fail. So: convergence first.

Set it up as a sequence.

x1=2,xn+1=2+xn.x_1 = \sqrt{2}, \qquad x_{n+1} = \sqrt{2 + x_n}.x1​=2​,xn+1​=2+xn​​.

The sequence is monotonically increasing, so by the Monotone Convergence Theorem it converges as long as it’s bounded above.

Bounded by 2, by induction. Base case: x1=2<2x_1 = \sqrt{2} < 2x1​=2​<2. Inductive step: if 2≤xn<2\sqrt{2} \le x_n < 22​≤xn​<2, then

xn+1=2+xn<2+2=2,xn+1=2+xn≥2.x_{n+1} = \sqrt{2 + x_n} < \sqrt{2 + 2} = 2, \qquad x_{n+1} = \sqrt{2 + x_n} \ge \sqrt{2}.xn+1​=2+xn​​<2+2​=2,xn+1​=2+xn​​≥2​.

So 2≤xn<2\sqrt{2} \le x_n < 22​≤xn​<2 for all nnn. Bounded and increasing means convergent, and now the fixed-point argument is legitimate: the limit LLL satisfies L=2+LL = \sqrt{2 + L}L=2+L​, whose positive root is

L=2.L = 2.L=2.

The trigonometric easter egg. There’s a lovely second way to see this via the half-angle identity 2cos⁡θ=2+2cos⁡2θ2\cos\theta = \sqrt{2 + 2\cos 2\theta}2cosθ=2+2cos2θ​. Unrolling it,

2cos⁡ ⁣(π2m+1)=2+2+⋯+2⏟m radicals,2\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2^{m+1}}\right) = \underbrace{\sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \cdots + \sqrt{2}}}}_{m \text{ radicals}},2cos(2m+1π​)=m radicals2+2+⋯+2​​​​​,

so the mmm-fold nested radical is exactly 2cos⁡(π/2m+1)2\cos(\pi/2^{m+1})2cos(π/2m+1). As m→∞m \to \inftym→∞ the angle goes to 0 and the expression goes to 2cos⁡(0)=22\cos(0) = 22cos(0)=2 — same answer, and as a bonus you get a closed form for every finite truncation, which is how those “compass-and-straightedge” formulas like 2cos⁡(π/16)=2+2+22\cos(\pi/16) = \sqrt{2+\sqrt{2+\sqrt{2}}}2cos(π/16)=2+2+2​​​ come about.

Two morals. First, “let LLL be the value and solve for it” is the last step of a limit argument, never the first. Second, when a recursion involves 2+(⋅)\sqrt{2 + (\cdot)}2+(⋅)​, there’s a decent chance a cosine is hiding in it.
